<!--
Curated reader manuscript v2.0 source.
chapter_id: capability-replacement-and-rollback
generated_baseline_ref: generated:54412328b6b9826f9c42de59ff7894c5a256dbc3ce5d852e5d7e0bcc2d8d82f0:chapters/capability-replacement-and-rollback.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/capability-replacement-and-rollback.qmd@cb7493ae57d5
This derivative preserves claim meaning, argument support, source/formal boundaries, implementation horizons, residuals, and non-claims.
No independent external-human review is claimed.
-->

# Capability Replacement and Rollback

Stable Capability Fields define the boundary that replacement must cross under review. Replacement is the transaction that crosses it. It is not swapping one component for another; it is a controlled change with compatibility checks, canary scope, monitoring, rollback, and residual ownership. The field says what must remain true; the transaction says whether the candidate earned the right to become ordinary infrastructure.

Reversibility is the trust boundary. A self-improving system should not be trusted because each new version sounds better, compiles faster, or wins a local comparison. It should be trusted only to the degree that failure can be detected, contained, explained, and undone. Until those records exist, improvement belongs in shadow, canary, quarantine, or research state rather than quietly becoming the default path.

Rollback turns ambition into an inspectable experiment instead of an irreversible bet. It preserves the last known accountable state while the candidate proves compatibility, safety envelope, and residual ownership. A replacement that cannot be unwound has already spent more trust than it earned. The right to say improvement failed is what keeps self-improvement from becoming self-ratification.

## Problem

Recursive improvement requires a safe procedure for replacing components while preserving identity, regression history, and recovery paths.

Stable capability fields define the socket. Replacement defines the transaction that changes what is plugged into it. A system that can improve itself needs a record of the prior implementation, candidate implementation, evidence gates, regressions, residuals, state migration, monitor window, and rollback path.

RMI and Benchmaxxing supply the ratchet discipline: move the frontier while preserving the floor. Cognitive Loop Closure adds lifecycle discipline for tools that emerge from repeated work. Talos adds job, audit, and replay expectations. SCF supplies the identity boundary that the replacement transaction must preserve. This is the first place where the book's improvement story becomes procedural: a better candidate is not accepted until the record can show what survived the change.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Ad hoc upgrades make it hard to know whether a system improved, drifted, lost a regression, or captured its evaluator.

An ad hoc upgrade can pass a visible benchmark and still delete a regression, change a field's semantics, invalidate a state migration, or make rollback impossible. It can also be self-judged: the component being replaced proposes, evaluates, and approves its own successor. That is not recursive improvement; it is evaluator capture with a success story.

Corrigibility provides the external baseline for why replacement needs a preserved correction path. `ext_corrigibility_2015` treats intervention tolerance and operator correction as properties that can be lost by design; a replacement transaction is the systems version of the same concern. A rollback record asks whether correction, regression floors, evaluator separation, and recovery handles survive the upgrade, without claiming that any real replacement has passed that bar.

Deployment practice supplies narrower rollout vocabulary but not the whole replacement argument. Kubernetes Deployments (`ext_kubernetes_deployments_docs`) make rollout history, revision records, and rollback to a prior stable revision familiar. Argo Rollouts (`ext_argo_rollouts_docs`) adds blue-green rollout, canary rollout, traffic shaping, metric analysis, automated promotion, and automated rollback as progressive-delivery concepts. Feature toggles (`ext_feature_toggles_fowler`) ground controlled exposure and canary cohorts. Google Cloud's MLOps continuous-delivery guidance (`ext_google_cloud_mlops_cd`) adds the ML-specific warning that model rollout involves code, data, schemas, model quality, serving pipelines, monitoring, and rollback triggers rather than ordinary software deployment alone. The ASI Stack borrows this operational discipline, then asks for stricter transaction fields: field identity, authority ceiling, evaluator independence, regression floors, residual ownership, rollback receipts, and support-state boundaries.

Replacement needs transaction semantics because partial success can be worse than failure. The system should know whether it is in precheck, gate, canary, commit, monitor, rollback, quarantine, or retirement.

The key reader-facing distinction is candidate improvement versus accepted replacement. A candidate can be promising, cheaper, faster, or cleaner without becoming the default. Accepted replacement means the stack has preserved field identity, checked authority boundaries, retained regression floors, recorded residuals, and kept a recovery path. Until that happens, the candidate belongs in shadow, canary, or quarantine state, not in the ordinary route.

Rollback also has to be more specific than nostalgia for the prior version. The prior artifact must still exist, the old state must be readable or compensated, external effects must be accounted for, and the monitor window must say when rollback is triggered. If any of those pieces is missing, the transaction can still record a partial recovery plan, but it should not claim reversible improvement.

One-way improvement creates the rollback gap. A candidate is allowed to replace the old path because the forward story is attractive, while the reverse path, state compatibility, dependent artifacts, rights preservation, and external effects are left implicit.

That one-way framing also hides who owns the risk. If a replacement fails, someone or something must know which artifact to restore, which state migration to reverse or compensate, which users or downstream routes were affected, and which residual remains unresolved. Without that owner, rollback is only a comforting word.

## Core Claim

Capability replacement should be an evidence-gated transaction with preconditions, regression checks, residual records, and rollback (evidence boundary: architectural argument).
SCF supplies field identity, RMI and Benchmaxxing supply regression/residual ratchet discipline, Cognitive Loop Closure supplies tool lifecycle pressure, and Talos supplies audit/replay context. Five local raw-cache mappings now have reviewed passage references, while `moecot` remains connector-only review context. External rollout and MLOps comparators ground vocabulary for canaries, feature flags, rollout history, model delivery, monitoring, and rollback triggers, but none of these sources is a reproduced replacement run in this repo.

## Mechanism

A replacement transaction is the moment an SCF stops being an abstract boundary and becomes an accountable change. It turns a proposed improvement into a sequence of prechecks, gates, canaries, residual decisions, and rollback obligations. The transaction is allowed to fail; what it cannot do is fail invisibly.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
A["Prior implementation"] --> B["Replacement proposal"]
B --> C["Wall diagnosis + evidence packet"]
C --> D["Precheck: field identity + authority"]
D --> E["Qualification gates"]
E --> F["Regression floor + holdouts"]
F --> R["Residual escrow"]
R --> G{"Pass with rollback ready?"}
G -- "yes" --> I["Canary / commit"]
G -- "no" --> H["Reject / quarantine / residual"]
I --> J["Monitor window"]
J --> K{"Incident or regression?"}
K -- "yes" --> L["Rollback"]
K -- "no" --> N["Promote / retain prior as recovery"]
H --> M["Evidence + changelog"]
L --> M
N --> M
```

**How to read the replacement transaction:** The replacement path is a reversible transaction, not a swap. Evidence, authority, qualification, regression floors, residual escrow, canary monitoring, and rollback all have to resolve before promotion can be recorded.

The replacement proposal is itself an artifact. It cannot become a commit merely because the candidate looks better. The transaction runs qualification, regression, authority, residual, and rollback checks before default promotion, and it records failed gates as residuals rather than letting them disappear behind a successful upgrade story.

The transaction names the transaction state, field, prior implementation, candidate, identity-preservation claim, gates, evidence refs, regression suite, evaluator-independence boundary, residual escrow, rollback command or plan, rollback receipt, approval record, canary scope, monitor window, monitor status, final decision, promotion blockers, source refs, support-state effect, and explicit non-claims. Failed gates become residuals rather than disappearing.

A good transaction preserves both sides of the change. It records why the candidate exists and why the prior implementation mattered. It keeps the regression suite attached so a future reviewer can see whether the floor was preserved. It keeps canary and monitor evidence separate from default promotion. It keeps rollback as an obligation, not a vague assurance. That is what turns recursive improvement from a mutation story into a governed state transition.

The passage review sharpens one boundary: rollback is not just "run the old version." A state migration can be replayable, loss-bounded, dual-read, or non-solvent. If the old state cannot be interpreted, or if external effects remain unresolved, the transaction should record a fork, compensation obligation, or residual effect rather than claiming full recovery.

Benchmarks are used as evidence surfaces, not permission slips. If a benchmark saturates, becomes gamed, or fails to transfer, that diagnosis belongs in the transaction.

The transaction should include a rollback receipt even when rollback is not exercised. The receipt records the prior artifact, state migration status, reversible fields, irreversible effects, dry-run status, trigger conditions, and owner. If rollback is impossible, the record should say compensation, fork, quarantine, or residual instead of pretending reversibility. A rollback plan without a receipt is a design intention; it is not rollback readiness.

The receipt is also what prevents replacement from erasing security and governance context. A candidate that preserves benchmark behavior but changes handle scope, audit retention, appeal paths, or review ownership is not the same replacement transaction. It has crossed into the security and authority boundary.

## Interfaces

The replacement transaction connects four ledgers. The SCF ledger says what identity must be preserved. The benchmark ledger says what frontier and regression evidence was used. The artifact graph says which candidate, state migration, and rollback materials exist. The changelog says what became default, what stayed canary, and what was rejected.

- SCF defines the field.
- Benchmarks test behavior.
- Evidence and changelog record the transition.

Minimum fields:

- `transaction_id`
- `transaction_state`
- `field_id`
- `prior_implementation`
- `candidate_implementation`
- `identity_preservation`
- `precheck_results`
- `qualification_evidence`
- `regression_results`
- `authority_check`
- `evaluator_independence`
- `residual_escrow`
- `rollback_plan`
- `rollback_receipt`
- `approval_record`
- `canary_scope`
- `monitor_window`
- `monitor_status`
- `decision`
- `promotion_blockers`
- `source_refs`
- `support_state_effect`
- `non_claims`

SCF defines the field. Benchmark ratchets and regression suites test behavior. Evidence and changelog records preserve the transition. Governance approves authority changes and high-impact commits.

The interface also protects later chapters. Security checks whether the replacement crosses a secret or permission boundary. Runtime adapters check whether the replacement changes effect authority. Policy optimization checks whether learning pressure is trying to ratify its own evaluator. The transaction gives each layer a concrete artifact to inspect rather than asking them to trust the word "upgrade."

Transaction and decision states need to be explicit: proposed, precheck, shadow, canary, default-candidate, committed, rolled-back, quarantined, superseded, retired, and blocked. These states keep a candidate from becoming default simply because it survived one test. The transaction should also name canary scope, monitor status, promotion blockers, and what the candidate is not allowed to conclude from the fixture: no runtime replacement, no rollback execution, no monitor-window success, and no support-state promotion unless separate evidence exists.

## Invariants

- No replacement without prior and posterior artifacts.
- Regressions stay attached.
- Rollback metadata is required before promotion.
- A rollback receipt must name reversible fields, irreversible effects, trigger conditions, and owner before default promotion.

The transaction must leave enough information to answer a simple question later: what changed, why was it allowed, what got worse, and how could the system return to the prior state?

Evaluator independence protects the replacement gate. The candidate may supply evidence, but it cannot be the sole evaluator, gatekeeper, or rollback authority for its own promotion.

Promotion also requires scope stability. A candidate can be accepted for one field, workload, permission tier, or context boundary without becoming the default everywhere. The transaction should preserve what was tested, what was not tested, which regressions were tolerated, and which rollback trigger still applies. A replacement that broadens its own scope during promotion is not an upgrade; it is an authority expansion.

## Failure modes

- Regression deletion.
- Rollback impossible after deployment.
- Self-judged replacement.

Regression deletion is the classic false improvement: the candidate does better on the frontier while forgetting what the stack already knew. Rollback impossibility turns a bad canary into permanent damage. Self-judged replacement lets a component rewrite the evidence used to approve it. Each failure blocks promotion.

Rollback theater is the replacement-layer failure that looks safest on paper. The transaction contains a rollback field, but no executable path, state-compatibility check, trigger condition, or responsible owner exists when the monitor window fails.

Frontier bias pushes the same transaction in the other direction. A candidate looks better on new tasks, so the system forgets old obligations that are less exciting but still required: rights preservation, cost ceilings, context restrictions, reproducibility, logging, and human approval behavior. Replacement review should make boring regressions as visible as impressive gains. Otherwise the stack ratchets toward novelty while quietly losing governability.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

Replacement should start as a `replacement_transaction` schema and valid fixture. It validates transaction state, identity preservation, required evidence, evaluator independence, residual escrow, rollback plan, rollback receipt, canary scope, monitor window/status, promotion blockers, source refs, support-state effect, decision, and non-claims, not a claim that a real replacement succeeded.

The current synthetic harness extends that slice to model-rollout records: canary, commit, and rollback examples must name data validation, schema validation, model-quality or model-identity checks, serving or prediction-service integration, monitor triggers, and baseline regression floors. Rollback receipts that admit irreversible or external effects must carry those effects into residuals, blockers, or non-claims and name a non-candidate owner.

A replacement trace should be the first moving piece: proposal, shadow run, canary with regression floor, failed rollback dry run that blocks default promotion, and a successful rollback receipt for a separate reversible candidate.

The Capability replacement trace probe at `experiments/capability_replacement_trace/results/2026-07-02-local.json` supplies that first bounded moving piece. It records a baseline implementation, a candidate kept in non-default canary state, a synthetic monitor trigger, rollback to the prior implementation, residual escrow, and three expected-invalid controls for authority widening, failed regression, and missing rollback. `python3 scripts/validate_capability_replacement_trace_probe.py` replays the trace summary, and `lean:replacement.transaction.trace_probe_bridge` mirrors its finite counts and boundaries.

The same trace now carries an identity sequence check. `lean:replacement.identity_sequence.invariant_bridge` records that the canary and rollback keep the same field identity, that a monitor failure blocks default promotion, that rollback restores the prior implementation rather than a convenient substitute, that the authority envelope remains unchanged, and that residual ownership stays present. Four sequence controls reject identity drift, monitor-failed defaulting, rollback-prior mismatch, and missing residual owner. This is still no deployed replacement execution, no production rollback, no monitor-quality result, no regression-suite-quality result, no model-rollout result, and no support-state transition.

That trace should keep boring regressions visible: cost, permissions, logging, context use, human review, and export/fork rights. A candidate that improves frontier task score while weakening any of those fields should remain canary-only or rejected until the regression is repaired.

The current formal slice models only finite records after replacement-review fields exist, but it now covers both transaction routing and lifecycle admission. It checks that missing artifacts reject the proposal; identity mismatches, failed regression floors, and failed regressions quarantine candidates; authority widening without governance and evaluator capture route to review; stale evidence, missing canary scope, missing monitor windows, missing rollback handles, and missing rollback receipts block promotion; failed canaries and failed rollback dry runs stay canary-only; monitor incidents require rollback; unowned irreversible effects and missing residual owners block admission; deprecation and retirement require notices or receipts; missing non-claim boundaries block promotion; and complete records can commit default. It does not run a real replacement, measure regression quality, observe a monitor window, or execute rollback.

## Beyond the State of the Art

Capability replacement only works as a transaction control plane for the whole stack. Every candidate implementation enters as a transaction, not a hope: prior artifact, candidate artifact, field identity, authority ceiling, regression floor, evaluator boundary, canary scope, monitor window, rollback receipt, and residual inheritance are all first-class objects.

In that end state, a capability can improve continuously without making the system forget what it used to know. Regressions follow the field forward, not the old implementation backward. Failed canaries become residuals. Rollbacks are rehearsed and owned. State migration is classified as reversible, compensatable, forked, or residual rather than treated as an implementation detail. A default route is therefore never just the latest winner; it is the candidate that survived the transaction while preserving the floor.

Beyond current practice, replacement would also be compositional. Security, governance rights, runtime adapters, benchmark ratchets, and self-improvement gates would all read the same transaction record. If a candidate changes audit retention, handle scope, appeal paths, verification budget, or rollback ownership, the transaction would surface that as a boundary delta before promotion.

The book should treat that product surface as a design destination rather than a reported upgrade pipeline. Promotion remains outside the present evidence boundary until replayable replacement traces, regression-preservation tests, rollback dry runs, monitor-window records, governance receipts, and failed-candidate examples exercise the transaction semantics under ordinary success and failure pressure.

## Post-v2.1 full-state rollback result

The update campaign makes rollback authority prospective and inventories 24
surfaces spanning model, optimizer, scheduler, RNG, caches, checkpoints,
backups, and descendant lineage. All fifteen seed-arm transactions return that
declared tree to its exact pre-update digest. Six arms nevertheless show that
the validation-selected checkpoint differs from the final checkpoint, and the
three unsafe comparator arms remain ineligible after crossing their retained-
task bound. This closes `UU-04` only for the declared local inventory. It does
not establish recovery of remote stores, external effects, live services, or
semantic behavior after production rollback. The separate governed-work
result, where only 32/36 attack-control rollbacks were exact, is the reminder
that completeness is inventory-specific rather than inherited from the word
“rollback.”

| Source | Title | Use and boundary |
|---|---|---|
| `ext_txfs_2018` | TxFS: Leveraging File-System Crash Consistency to Provide ACID Transactions | Transactional-filesystem comparator for write-set, recovery, and crash-consistency boundaries; not evidence that ASI Stack external effects are transactional. |

## Summary

Capability Replacement and Rollback turns improvement into a transaction. It preserves the prior state, candidate evidence, regressions, residuals, approval, monitor window, and rollback path.

The governing discipline is floor preservation. The frontier may move only when the existing floor remains visible and recoverable.

The security kernel keeps replacement from becoming a secret, permission, or context leak. Rollback is not meaningful if the candidate can carry privileged material across a boundary that the prior capability was never allowed to cross. Replacement must preserve not only behavior, but also the permission shape around that behavior.

A replacement should be easier to undo than to overclaim. If the record cannot identify the prior artifact, compatibility boundary, rollback trigger, and owner, the candidate is not ready to become default.
The mature habit is reversible progress: every promoted capability should leave enough evidence, routing context, and authority history for a later steward to understand why the change happened and how to recover if it fails.

## Handoff

Replacement becomes dangerous when it moves secrets, permissions, or privileged context across a boundary that the old capability was not allowed to cross. Security Kernel and Digital SCIFs follows because rollback and qualification are not enough if authority-bearing material can leak through logs, prompts, summaries, memory, or tool adapters. Protected power must become handle-bound, auditable, and absent by default.
